topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery opening was a whirlwind of polite chatter and clinking glasses. I stood near my latest piece, a vibrant abstract canvas I’d poured weeks of my soul into. Then I saw it. A small, almost hidden detail on the corner of the fabric, a delicate, intricate line work that wasn't mine. My breath hitched. I cautiously moved closer, my fingers trembling as I reached out. It was a tattoo, undeniably, and it was a reproduction of my signature motif, rendered in stark black ink on the sleeve of the person standing beside me. A flush crept up my neck, heating my cheeks until they felt tight and prickly. I quickly looked away, focusing intensely on a nearby sculpture, trying to will the sensation away. The silence in my head was deafening.

[story 2]

I was packing up my studio late one Tuesday, the scent of turpentine heavy in the air. As I was carefully wrapping my portfolio, my eyes snagged on the back of a discarded sketchpad. There, inked onto the skin of a hand resting on the page, was a perfect, miniature rendition of my most recognizable recurring symbol. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I snatched the pad, my movements jerky and hasty. My palms grew slick. Who? How could someone have done that? I frantically flipped through the rest of the pages, searching for context, but all I found was that strange, permanent mark. I shoved the pad into my bag, the weight of it suddenly unbearable, and hurried out of the room, needing the anonymity of the street.

[story 3]

It happened during a casual coffee break at a local art fair. I was admiring a large charcoal portrait when I noticed a subtle, almost hidden detail on the artist’s wrist—a stylized swirl that mirrored the core design of my most famous series. My entire posture shifted; my shoulders drew inward, and I gripped my lukewarm cup so tightly my knuckles turned white. I forced myself to approach the artist, my voice coming out a little too high-pitched. "Excuse me," I managed, trying to keep my tone neutral. When they looked up, their eyes met mine, and for a split second, I saw a flicker of recognition, followed by a swift, almost imperceptible tightening around their jaw. They quickly looked away, fiddling with their napkin. I mumbled an apology and retreated before I could say anything else, the heat rising in my chest making my skin feel too exposed.
